schema.howto_name: How to Tell If Clear Quartz Is Real

howto_steps.name: Check for bubbles with a 10x loupe
howto_steps.text: Round bubbles mean glass; natural clear quartz has irregular inclusions (needles, veils) or none.

howto_steps.name: Hardness test on rough stones only
howto_steps.text: Genuine quartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass; glass imitations cannot. Never use on polished jewelry.

howto_steps.name: Temperature and heft
howto_steps.text: Genuine quartz feels cool and has the right density (SG 2.65); plastic and some glass feel lighter.

howto_steps.name: Spot the naming trap
howto_steps.text: 'Crystal quartz' sold cheaply is often glass — natural quartz is properly called 'quartz.'

howto_steps.name: Distinguish the three tiers
howto_steps.text: Natural (minor inclusions) vs clarified/reconstituted (too perfect) vs pure glass (bubbles, flow lines).

compliance.gentle_note: Clear quartz identification tests combine practical mineralogy (bubbles, hardness, density) with marketplace awareness. Because clear quartz is affordable, the fakes tend to be cheap glass beads sold in bulk — but a gemological lab can confirm any high-value specimen. Crystal meanings are separate from authenticity.
compliance.brand_tagline: We name our stones and sources — return to what's real.
